hook: You already know how to write down a transition matrix for a Markov chain and compute multi-step probabilities. But here is something that transition matrix cannot immediately tell you: if the chain starts in state 3, can it ever reach state 7, and if so, can it ever come back? Some states are isolated islands; others belong to tightly connected groups. Without a way to classify this structure, you cannot predict long-run behavior at all. So the question is: how do we carve the state space of a Markov chain into meaningful groups based on which states can reach which?
concept: Two states communicate if each can reach the other in a finite number of steps. Formally, states $i$ and $j$ communicate, written $i \leftrightarrow j$, if there exist integers $m, n \geq 1$ such that $P^m(i,j) > 0$ and $P^n(j,i) > 0$. Communication is an equivalence relation, so it partitions the entire state space into disjoint communication classes. A Markov chain is called irreducible if every pair of states communicates, meaning the whole state space forms a single class.
anatomy: The key structural pieces are: the accessibility relation '$i \to j$' (state $i$ can reach state $j$, meaning $P^n(i,j) > 0$ for some $n \geq 1$), the communication relation '$i \leftrightarrow j$' (accessibility holds in both directions), and communication classes (the equivalence classes produced by $\leftrightarrow$, which partition the state space $S$). Within the class structure, a class $C$ is called closed if no state in $C$ can access any state outside $C$ (formally, $i \in C$ and $i \to j$ implies $j \in C$), and open otherwise. An irreducible chain has exactly one class, which must be closed.

worked_example.description_latex: We begin by writing down the transition matrix and labeling the states. Each entry $P(i,j)$ gives the one-step probability of moving from row-state $i$ to column-state $j$, and we will use these entries to build a directed graph that makes the accessibility structure visible.

worked_example.expression_latex: 1 \to 2 \text{ (since } P(1,2)=1>0\text{)}, \quad 2 \to 1 \text{ (since } P(2,1)=1/2>0\text{)}, \quad 3 \to 1, 3 \to 2, 3 \to 4 \text{ (row 3 is positive in all columns)}, \quad 4 \to 4 \text{ only}
worked_example.description_latex: We check each row for positive entries to determine one-step accessibility. Notice that state 4 has $P(4,4)=1$ and all other entries zero, so from state 4 you can never leave — this is an immediate signal that $\{4\}$ is a closed class by itself.

worked_example.expression_latex: 1 \leftrightarrow 2 \text{ because } 1 \to 2 \text{ and } 2 \to 1. \quad 3 \to 1 \text{ but } 1 \not\to 3 \text{ (rows 1 and 2 have zero in column 3)}, \text{ so } 3 \not\leftrightarrow 1.
worked_example.description_latex: To check communication we need two-way accessibility. States 1 and 2 communicate because the chain can travel in both directions in one step. State 3 can reach states 1 and 2, but neither row 1 nor row 2 has any positive probability of moving to state 3, so state 3 cannot be reached from $\{1, 2\}$, breaking the two-way requirement.

We collect the equivalence classes. States 1 and 2 form one class, state 3 is alone in its own class (it cannot communicate with any other state), and state 4 is alone in its own class. This gives us three distinct communication classes partitioning $S$.

worked_example.expression_latex: C_1 = \{1,2\}: \text{ open (3 can enter } C_1\text{, but states in }C_1\text{ cannot reach 3 or 4)}. \quad C_2=\{3\}: \text{ open (3 can leave to 1,2,4)}. \quad C_3=\{4\}: \text{ closed (4 cannot leave)}. \quad \text{Chain is NOT irreducible.}
worked_example.description_latex: Finally we classify each communication class as open or closed. A class is closed when no arrow leaves it; here only $\{4\}$ is closed because row 4 never exits. Because the state space does not consist of a single class, the chain is not irreducible — starting in state 3, the chain can drift into $\{1,2\}$ or get absorbed into state 4, and these two fates cannot be reversed.

partial_example.description_latex: We are given a three-state chain. Your first task is to read off which states are accessible from which by identifying every positive entry in each row of $P$.

Here is the full list of one-step accessibility arrows. Notice that every state has a direct path to every other state in one step. Now use this to determine the communication classes: which states communicate with which, and is the chain irreducible?

Since every pair of states can reach each other in one step, state $i \leftrightarrow j$ for all $i,j \in \{1,2,3\}$. Write down the communication classes and state whether this chain is irreducible, giving a one-sentence justification.
Is the single class you identified open or closed? Explain by checking whether any transition exits the class, then state what irreducibility implies about the class being closed.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A chain has transition matrix $P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ on $S=\{1,2\}$. Find all communication classes and state whether the chain is irreducible.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Classes: $\{1\}$ and $\{2\}$, both closed. The chain is NOT irreducible because the two states cannot reach each other.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A four-state chain has the following non-zero transitions: $P(1,2)>0$, $P(2,3)>0$, $P(3,1)>0$, $P(4,1)>0$, and $P(4,4)>0$, with all other entries zero. Identify all communication classes and classify each as open or closed.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Class $\{1,2,3\}$ is closed (no exit exists); class $\{4\}$ is open because $4 \to 1$ allows leaving. The chain is not irreducible.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $S=\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ with transitions $1\to 2$, $2\to 1$, $2\to 3$, $3\to 4$, $4\to 3$, $5\to 1$ (all positive, all other entries zero). Find all communication classes.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Classes: $\{1,2\}$ (open, since $2\to 3$ exits), $\{3,4\}$ (closed), $\{5\}$ (open). The chain is not irreducible.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A chain on $S=\{1,2,3\}$ has $P(1,2) = p$, $P(1,1)=1-p$, $P(2,3)=1$, $P(3,1)=1$ for some $0 < p \leq 1$. Find the communication classes. Then use the fact that a finite irreducible chain always has a unique stationary distribution to determine whether a unique stationary distribution $\pi$ exists here.
practice_problems.answer_latex: All states communicate: $1\to 2\to 3\to 1$ and every reverse path is achievable in multiple steps, giving one class $\{1,2,3\}$. The chain is irreducible, so a unique stationary distribution exists.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A gambler plays a game starting with $\$2$. At each step she wins $\$1$ with probability $1/2$ and loses $\$1$ with probability $1/2$. The game ends if she reaches $\$0$ (broke) or $\$4$ (goal). Model this as a Markov chain on $S=\{0,1,2,3,4\}$ and identify the communication classes, specifying which are closed.
practice_problems.answer_latex: States $\{1,2,3\}$ form one open class (they communicate with each other and can exit to 0 or 4). States $\{0\}$ and $\{4\}$ are each their own closed (absorbing) classes. The chain is not irreducible.

common_mistakes: Students often confuse accessibility '$i \to j$' with communication '$i \leftrightarrow j$', concluding that two states communicate simply because $P^n(i,j)>0$ for some $n$, without verifying the reverse direction. Always check both $P^m(i,j)>0$ and $P^n(j,i)>0$ before claiming two states belong to the same class.

Students frequently look only at one-step probabilities when determining whether $i$ can reach $j$, missing multi-step paths. If $P(i,j)=0$ but $P(i,k)>0$ and $P(k,j)>0$, then $i \to j$ via state $k$ in two steps; always check $P^n$ for $n \geq 2$ before concluding inaccessibility.

Students sometimes label a communication class as 'closed' simply because it contains an absorbing state, without checking every state in the class. A class is closed only if no state anywhere in the class has a positive probability of transitioning to any state outside the class; verify every row corresponding to states in the class.
untested_variants: Determining communication classes for a chain defined on a countably infinite state space (e.g., a random walk on the non-negative integers with a reflecting barrier), where the partition cannot be found by exhaustive row inspection.

Given a communication class structure, reconstructing a valid transition matrix consistent with specified open/closed classifications and a prescribed stationary distribution — combining irreducibility with stationarity in reverse.

Using the concept of period alongside communication classes: verifying that all states within a communication class share the same period, and computing that period from the greatest common divisor of return times.

Analyzing communication classes in a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) defined by a generator (rate) matrix $Q$, where accessibility is determined by off-diagonal entries of $Q$ rather than entries of a transition matrix $P$.
